confidence.disclosure: Al-Kindi is the first major Arab philosopher and was patronised by three Abbasid caliphs, but no contemporary biography survives. We know his Kindi tribal lineage, his father's role as governor of Kufa, his Baghdad career, and his fall from favour under al-Mutawakkil — but the specific dates of these events come from inference and from the fragmentary list of his ~270 works in Ibn al-Nadim's Fihrist. Treat all dates outside Baghdad as approximate.

sources: Ibn Abi Usaybi'a, Uyun al-Anba (13th c.)

journey.event: Born in Kufa around this time into the noble Arab tribe of Kinda. His father Ishaq is the Abbasid governor of Kufa; the family traces its descent to the pre-Islamic kings of Kinda in southern Arabia.
Loses his father in childhood and grows up in the well-to-do Kindi household in Kufa, receiving the standard Arabic literary and Quranic education of an aristocratic boy.
Continues his higher studies in Basra, then the leading centre of Arabic grammar and theology, before moving on to the new imperial capital.
Settles in Baghdad. Caliph al-Ma'mun's House of Wisdom is in full operation, and al-Kindi joins the circle of translators rendering Greek philosophy and science into Arabic.

Caliph al-Mu'tasim succeeds al-Ma'mun. Al-Kindi enjoys high favour and is appointed tutor to the caliph's son Ahmad. He dedicates his Fi'l-Falsafa al-Ula (On First Philosophy) to al-Mu'tasim — the first systematic philosophical treatise in Arabic.
Follows the caliph when al-Mu'tasim moves the imperial capital from Baghdad to the new city of Samarra on the Tigris. Al-Kindi's relationship with the court is at its peak.

Produces the bulk of his philosophical and scientific output during these years: works on metaphysics, ethics, optics, music, meteorology, medicine, and what may be the earliest treatise on cryptanalysis (frequency analysis of encrypted Arabic text).
Caliph al-Mutawakkil takes power. The new ruler is hostile to the Mu'tazila rationalism that al-Kindi had embraced, and the philosopher's standing at court begins to erode.

Falls from favour. Court rivals — by some accounts the Banu Musa brothers, the famous mechanical engineers — engineer his disgrace; the caliph orders him beaten and his library confiscated. The library is later returned.
Spends his later years quietly in Baghdad, continuing to write and teach in private rather than at court. His pupil Ahmad ibn al-Tayyib al-Sarakhsi later carries his philosophical method forward.
Dies in Baghdad around this time, alone and out of favour according to Ibn Abi Usaybi'a. His exact death date is uncertain; later sources place it in the early 870s.
